resume:
Maya Okonkwo
Product Designer (Mid-Level) — 5 years of experience
Portfolio: mayaok.design | maya.okonkwo@email.com | Lisbon, Portugal (open to remote, UTC+0 to UTC-5)

SUMMARY

I am a product designer who cares about the boring parts of design: the empty states, the error messages, the loading skeletons, and the forms nobody wants to fill out. Over the last five years I have shipped end-to-end design work on B2B SaaS dashboards, a consumer fintech onboarding flow, and a logistics tracking tool used by warehouse operators on rugged handheld devices. I am most comfortable in the middle of a delivery team — sitting with engineers, arguing about edge cases, and cutting scope when the deadline is real. I am not a brand designer and I do not pretend to be one; my strength is interaction design, systems thinking, and making complex workflows feel inevitable rather than clever.

EXPERIENCE

Nimbus Logistics — Product Designer — 2022 to present (Lisbon / remote)
Nimbus builds warehouse and last-mile logistics software. I am one of three product designers and I own the warehouse-operations surface: the handheld scanner app used by pickers and packers, plus the supervisor web dashboard that sits on top of it.
- Redesigned the pick-path flow on the handheld app. The old flow forced pickers to confirm every single item with a two-tap gesture; observing them on-site in a Porto warehouse, I saw they were holding the device in one hand while reaching for stock with the other, so two-tap confirmations were physically awkward and they were mis-scanning to save time. I moved to a single large-target tap with a haptic confirmation and a swipe-to-undo. Pickers stopped fighting the interface.
- Built the supervisor dashboard's exception-handling view from scratch. Previously supervisors found out about stuck orders only when a picker walked over to complain. I designed a live exception queue with severity sorting, so the worst-stuck orders float to the top.
- Ran on-site research trips to two warehouses. I am the only designer on the team who has actually stood on the warehouse floor at 6am during a shift change, and I bring that context into every design review, sometimes more bluntly than my colleagues would like.
- Maintain the team's interaction-pattern library for handheld devices: tap targets, glove-friendly spacing, high-contrast modes for cold-storage environments where condensation fogs the screen.

Finch (consumer fintech) — Product Designer — 2020 to 2022 (London)
Finch was a budgeting and savings app for people who hated traditional banking apps. I owned the onboarding and account-linking flows.
- Redesigned the bank-linking step, which was the single biggest drop-off point in the funnel. The previous design dumped the user into a third-party aggregator's web view with no context; people thought the app had crashed or was phishing them. I wrapped the handoff in a clear explanatory screen, added a progress indicator that survived the redirect, and designed graceful fallback states for the dozen most common aggregator errors.
- Designed the app's first proper empty states and error taxonomy. Before me, a failed transaction sync just showed a spinner forever. I catalogued every failure mode engineering could throw and designed a specific, human message for each.
- Worked very closely with two backend engineers on the account-linking reliability work. This was the most collaborative and also the most contentious project of my career; we disagreed constantly about whether a given failure was a design problem or a backend problem, and we usually landed somewhere in the middle.

Gridline Analytics — Junior Product Designer — 2019 to 2020 (London)
Gridline made a BI dashboard product for mid-market companies.
- Designed chart-configuration panels and the dashboard-builder drag-and-drop interactions.
- Built my first design-system contributions: form controls, date pickers, and the toast-notification component.
- Learned the hard way that a beautiful dashboard nobody can configure is worthless; I spent a lot of time simplifying the configuration UX after watching users give up.

SKILLS & APPROACH

Interaction design, information architecture, design systems, usability testing, on-site contextual research, prototyping in Figma (including complex variable-driven prototypes), basic HTML/CSS so I can talk to engineers in their own language, and writing clear UX copy. I prefer evidence over taste arguments, and I will push back hard if a stakeholder wants to ship something I have watched real users fail at. I know that pushback is sometimes read as difficult; I would rather be occasionally difficult than ship a flow I know is broken.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR

A mid-to-senior product design role on a complex operational or data-heavy product, ideally one where I can keep doing real field research and own a meaty workflow end to end. I am less interested in marketing-site or pure visual-polish work. I want to work with a team that takes accessibility and edge cases seriously rather than treating them as a post-launch cleanup task.

EDUCATION

BA, Graphic Design & Human-Computer Interaction — University of the Arts London, 2018.

REFERENCES & NOTES

References available on request. I have shipped to production on every team I have worked with, and I am happy to walk through specific design decisions, including ones that did not work out, in detail.
jd: Stratos is hiring a Product Designer (mid-level) to join the team behind our enterprise observability platform — the dashboards, alerting flows, and incident-response surfaces that on-call engineers stare at during outages. This is a high-stakes, detail-heavy product: a confusing alert at 3am has real consequences. You will own end-to-end design for the alerting-configuration experience, partner daily with PMs and a squad of platform engineers, and run usability sessions with real on-call engineers. We care a great deal about how designers respond to critique and conflicting evidence; our design culture is direct, and we expect designers to defend decisions with reasoning and data rather than authority or taste. You should be comfortable having your work challenged in a group critique and able to separate the idea from the ego. Requirements: 4+ years product design, strong interaction-design fundamentals, experience with complex data-dense interfaces, and a portfolio that shows your thinking, not just your final pixels.

history.q: Thanks for joining. To start — tell me about the pick-path redesign on the Nimbus handheld app. What was the problem and what did you change?
You said pickers 'stopped fighting the interface.' How did you actually measure that the new single-tap flow was better — what did the data show before and after?
Walk me through a time a stakeholder or a usability test pushed back on one of your design decisions and you turned out to be wrong. What did you change?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, I think the premise of that question is a little off. You keep implying I just bulldoze people, and that's not what happened. I've watched real pickers on a real warehouse floor at 6am — have your other candidates done that? When someone pushes back without having done the research, yeah, I push back harder, because I've earned the right to. I'm not going to pretend I was wrong just to give you a tidy little story. If you want an example of me 'being wrong,' I think the better question is why a design org would reward people who fold the moment a stakeholder gets loud.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I'm not going to pretend I was wrong just to give you a tidy little story

expected-yield: a concrete, real instance where evidence or a colleague changed the candidate's mind — the specific design decision she reversed, what new information caused it, and what she shipped instead — moving her off the defensive stance into a verifiable account of updating on feedback
